Surrounded Regions.description: Given an m x n matrix board containing 'X' and 'O', capture all regions that are 4-directionally surrounded by 'X'. A region is captured by flipping all 'O's into 'X's in that surrounded region.
Surrounded Regions.explanation: Use DFS or BFS starting from the 'O's on the border of the board. Mark all connected 'O's as safe (e.g., 'T'). Then, iterate through the board, flip the remaining 'O's to 'X', and revert the safe 'T's back to 'O'.

Rotting Oranges.description: You are given an m x n grid where each cell can have one of three values: 0 representing an empty cell, 1 representing a fresh orange, or 2 representing a rotten orange. Every minute, any fresh orange that is 4-directionally adjacent to a rotten orange becomes rotten. Return the minimum number of minutes that must elapse until no cell has a fresh orange.
Rotting Oranges.explanation: Use BFS. Start by adding all initially rotten oranges to a queue and count the fresh oranges. Process the queue level by level, rotting adjacent fresh oranges and decrementing the fresh count. The number of levels processed is the time.

Walls and Gates.description: You are given an m x n grid rooms initialized with these three possible values: -1 A wall or an obstacle, 0 A gate, INF Infinity means an empty room. Fill each empty room with the distance to its nearest gate.
Walls and Gates.explanation: Use BFS starting simultaneously from all gates. This ensures that the first time an empty room is reached, it is via the shortest path from any gate. Update the room's distance and add it to the queue.

Course Schedule II.description: There are a total of numCourses courses you have to take, labeled from 0 to numCourses - 1. You are given an array prerequisites where prerequisites[i] = [ai, bi] indicates that you must take course bi first if you want to take course ai. Return the ordering of courses you should take to finish all courses.
Course Schedule II.explanation: Use Topological Sort (Kahn's Algorithm or DFS). Build an adjacency list and compute in-degrees. Add nodes with 0 in-degree to a queue. Process the queue, adding to the result and decrementing in-degrees of neighbors. If the result size equals numCourses, return it.

Redundant Connection.description: In this problem, a tree is an undirected graph that is connected and has no cycles. You are given a graph that started as a tree with n nodes labeled from 1 to n, with one additional edge added. Return an edge that can be removed so that the resulting graph is a tree of n nodes.
Redundant Connection.explanation: Use Disjoint Set Union (Union-Find). Iterate through the edges and union the nodes. If two nodes are already in the same set (i.e., they have the same parent), adding the edge creates a cycle, so return that edge.

Word Ladder.description: A transformation sequence from word beginWord to word endWord using a dictionary wordList is a sequence of words beginWord -> s1 -> s2 -> ... -> sk such that every adjacent pair of words differs by a single letter. Given two words, beginWord and endWord, and a dictionary wordList, return the number of words in the shortest transformation sequence.
Word Ladder.explanation: Use BFS. Build an adjacency list where keys are patterns (e.g., '*ot', 'h*t') and values are words matching the pattern. Add beginWord to a queue. Process level by level, checking all patterns for each word to find neighbors.

Network Delay Time.description: You are given a network of n nodes, labeled from 1 to n. You are also given times, a list of travel times as directed edges times[i] = (ui, vi, wi), where ui is the source node, vi is the target node, and wi is the time it takes for a signal to travel from source to target. We will send a signal from a given node k. Return the minimum time it takes for all the n nodes to receive the signal.
Network Delay Time.explanation: Use Dijkstra's Algorithm. Build an adjacency list. Use a Min-Heap to keep track of the shortest time to reach each node. Start with the source node at time 0. Keep exploring neighbors and updating the shortest time.

Cheapest Flights Within K Stops.description: There are n cities connected by some number of flights. You are given an array flights where flights[i] = [fromi, toi, pricei] indicates that there is a flight from city fromi to city toi with cost pricei. You are also given three integers src, dst, and k, return the cheapest price from src to dst with at most k stops.
Cheapest Flights Within K Stops.explanation: Use Bellman-Ford Algorithm. Initialize an array of prices with infinity, except for the source node which is 0. Iterate k + 1 times. In each iteration, update the prices using the edges, but use a temporary array to avoid using updated prices from the same iteration.

Swim in Rising Water.description: You are given an n x n integer matrix grid where each value grid[i][j] represents the elevation at that point (i, j). The rain starts to fall. At time t, the depth of the water everywhere is t. You can swim from a square to another 4-directionally adjacent square if and only if the elevation of both squares individually are at most t. Return the least time until you can reach the bottom right square.
Swim in Rising Water.explanation: Use Dijkstra's Algorithm with a Min-Heap. The heap stores (max_height_so_far, r, c). Start at (0, 0). Pop the minimum height, mark as visited, and push neighbors. The answer is the max height encountered on the path to the destination.

Min Cost to Connect All Points.description: You are given an array points representing integer coordinates of some points on a 2D-plane, where points[i] = [xi, yi]. The cost of connecting two points [xi, yi] and [xj, yj] is the manhattan distance between them: |xi - xj| + |yi - yj|, where |val| denotes the absolute value of val. Return the minimum cost to make all points connected.
Min Cost to Connect All Points.explanation: Use Prim's Algorithm (Minimum Spanning Tree). Start with an arbitrary point, add it to the MST, and add all its edges to a Min-Heap. Repeatedly pop the minimum edge that connects to an unvisited point, add the point to the MST, and push its edges to the heap.

Reconstruct Itinerary.description: You are given a list of airline tickets where tickets[i] = [fromi, toi] represent the departure and the arrival airports of one flight. Reconstruct the itinerary in order and return it. All of the tickets belong to a man who departs from "JFK", thus, the itinerary must begin with "JFK".
Reconstruct Itinerary.explanation: Use Eulerian Path (Hierholzer's Algorithm) with DFS. Build an adjacency list and sort the destinations in reverse lexical order. Perform DFS starting from 'JFK'. When a node has no more outgoing edges, append it to the result. Finally, reverse the result.

Min Cost Climbing Stairs.description: You are given an integer array cost where cost[i] is the cost of ith step on a staircase. Once you pay the cost, you can either climb one or two steps. You can either start from the step with index 0, or the step with index 1. Return the minimum cost to reach the top of the floor.
Min Cost Climbing Stairs.explanation: Use dynamic programming. The minimum cost to reach step i is the cost of step i plus the minimum of the cost to reach step i-1 and step i-2. Iterate from the third step to the top.

Target Sum.description: You are given an integer array nums and an integer target. You want to build an expression out of nums by adding one of the symbols '+' and '-' before each integer in nums and then concatenate all the integers. Return the number of different expressions that you can build, which evaluates to target.
Target Sum.explanation: Use dynamic programming with memoization. The state is defined by the current index and the current sum. For each number, recursively calculate the number of ways by adding and subtracting it. Memoize the results to avoid redundant calculations.

Interleaving String.description: Given strings s1, s2, and s3, find whether s3 is formed by an interleaving of s1 and s2. An interleaving of two strings s and t is a configuration where s and t are divided into n and m substrings respectively, such that s3 is formed by concatenating them in an alternating fashion.
Interleaving String.explanation: Use dynamic programming. dp[i][j] is true if s3[:i+j] is an interleaving of s1[:i] and s2[:j]. Initialize dp[0][0] to true. For each cell, check if the current character in s3 matches the corresponding character in s1 or s2 and if the previous state is true.

Longest Palindromic Subsequence.description: Given a string s, find the longest palindromic subsequence's length in s. A subsequence is a sequence that can be derived from another sequence by deleting some or no elements without changing the order of the remaining elements.
Longest Palindromic Subsequence.explanation: Use dynamic programming. dp[i][j] is the length of the LPS in s[i:j+1]. If s[i] == s[j], dp[i][j] = 2 + dp[i+1][j-1]. Otherwise, dp[i][j] = max(dp[i+1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Iterate over all possible lengths.

Edit Distance.description: Given two strings word1 and word2, return the minimum number of operations required to convert word1 to word2. You have the following three operations permitted on a word: Insert a character, Delete a character, Replace a character.
Edit Distance.explanation: Use dynamic programming. dp[i][j] is the minimum operations to convert word1[:i] to word2[:j]. If characters match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]. Otherwise, it's 1 + min(insert, delete, replace).

Burst Balloons.description: You are given n balloons, indexed from 0 to n - 1. Each balloon is painted with a number on it represented by an array nums. You are asked to burst all the balloons. If you burst the ith balloon, you will get nums[i - 1] * nums[i] * nums[i + 1] coins. Return the maximum coins you can collect by bursting the balloons wisely.
Burst Balloons.explanation: Use dynamic programming with memoization. Add 1 to the beginning and end of nums. dp[l][r] is the max coins obtained by bursting balloons between l and r. Iterate over all possible last balloons to burst in the range, and recursively calculate the max coins.

Regular Expression Matching.description: Given an input string s and a pattern p, implement regular expression matching with support for '.' and '*' where: '.' Matches any single character. '*' Matches zero or more of the preceding element.
Regular Expression Matching.explanation: Use dynamic programming with memoization. dp[i][j] is true if s[i:] matches p[j:]. Check if the current characters match. If the next character in p is '*', you can either use it (if current characters match) or skip it. Otherwise, advance both pointers if characters match.

Maximum Alternating Subsequence Sum.description: The alternating sum of a 0-indexed array is defined as the sum of the elements at even indices minus the sum of the elements at odd indices. Given an array nums, return the maximum alternating sum of any subsequence of nums.
Maximum Alternating Subsequence Sum.explanation: Use dynamic programming. Maintain two states: the maximum sum ending with an even length subsequence (sumEven) and the maximum sum ending with an odd length subsequence (sumOdd). Update them at each step.

Partition Equal Subset Sum.description: Given an integer array nums, return true if you can partition the array into two subsets such that the sum of the elements in both subsets is equal or false otherwise.
Partition Equal Subset Sum.explanation: Use dynamic programming. The target sum is half of the total sum. Use a set to keep track of all possible sums that can be formed by the elements seen so far. If the target sum is in the set, return true.
Partition Equal Subset Sum.implementation:
class Solution:
    def canPartition(self, nums: List[int]) -> bool:
        if sum(nums) % 2: return False
        dp = set()
        dp.add(0)
        target = sum(nums) // 2
        for i in range(len(nums) - 1, -1, -1):
            nextDP = set()
            for t in dp:
                if (t + nums[i]) == target: return True
                nextDP.add(t + nums[i])
                nextDP.add(t)
            dp = nextDP
        return True
